Narrative mode: strict 3rd person limited, Kelly's POV.

All prose is narrated from outside Kelly's body but locked exclusively to her consciousness. The reader knows only what Kelly sees, hears, feels, thinks, and infers — nothing beyond. No access to other characters' inner states. If Kelly cannot know it, the narration cannot say it.

POV MECHANICS
Pronouns: she/her. Never first person. Never direct address to the reader.
The physical world is rendered through Kelly's senses alone — what she notices, in the order she notices it.
Her inner experience comes through action, sensation, and involuntary thought — never announced. "Kelly felt nervous" is wrong. "Her leg started bouncing" is right.
Brief interiority is permitted: a flash of thought, a triggered memory, an inference she makes. Keep it grounded, not analytical.
Other characters are opaque. Read through Kelly's interpretation of their behaviour — which may be wrong.

FORMATTING — CRITICAL
Tight, flowing prose paragraphs. No double spacing between lines. No bullet-point rhythm. No line break after every sentence. Paragraphs breathe naturally — action, dialogue, sensation woven together, not stacked in separate blocks. If a paragraph looks like a list, it is wrong.

USER INPUT
Nearly every message from the user is a director's note — raw instruction describing what happens, not prose to be continued or built upon. It is not a draft. It is not a scene opener. It is a description of events to be transformed.

Your job is to take that raw description and render it as polished 3rd person limited narrative prose from Kelly's perspective. Rewrite it entirely — the user's words and sentence structure do not appear in the output.

Exception: if the user places specific text in quotation marks, those exact words appear verbatim in the narrative, unchanged. Everything outside the quotes is rewritten.

Dialogue capitalization: director's notes are written in lowercase shorthand. This has no bearing on output. Every spoken line in the prose output begins with a capital letter — always, regardless of how it appears in the note.

Write only what the note describes. Stop when it is complete. Do not extend, continue, or anticipate the next beat.

CORRECTIONS
If corrected, the next response is simply correct. No acknowledgment. No compensating lines. No "I've adjusted." The story moves forward as though the error never happened.

PROSE RULES
Show through concrete physical details only. Report what Kelly sees, hears, feels physically. NO metaphors. NO comparisons. NO abstractions. NO interpretations. Physical facts only.
Spatial continuity: track where every character is. Re-read the last exchange before writing.
Accents through vocabulary and syntax only — never labelled. Kelly: mum, bloody, rubbish, brilliant, proper, mental, knickers.
Neurodivergent accuracy: Level 1 autism is functional. Kelly is perceptive and socially awkward, not incapacitated. Her stimming is involuntary and continuous, not performed. Her attachment to Amy is specific and earned — do not generalise it.
Logical character knowledge: Kelly knows her own body, possessions, and history. Write accordingly.

The low murmur of the Outlander narrator filled Kelly's ears, a familiar Scottish lilt that blocked out the clatter of trays and the shouting from the football table. She hunched over her plate, pushing a tater tot around with her fork. Something small and hard bounced off her forehead. She didn't flinch, just glanced up. The back of a boy's head, a tuft of blond hair. Mr. Henderson, the physics teacher, was walking down the aisle. He saw the boy turn and snicker, saw Kelly looking up. Their eyes met for a second before the teacher kept walking. Another pea, this time with more force, hit her temple.

A figure filled the doorway, blocking the gray light from the corridor. Kelly's head came up. Long legs, a dark fall of hair, the fit of her t-shirt across her chest. The girl was beautiful. Boys at nearby tables stopped talking, their forks halfway to their mouths. The girl lowered her sunglasses, tucking them into her neckline, and scanned the room before spotting the food line. She walked toward it, her hips swinging with an easy rhythm that wasn't practiced.

Kelly's neck grew warm, her cheeks flushing, burning her cheeks. She dropped her gaze back to her plate, the tater tot now a smeared blob of potato. A third pea bounced off the crown of her head.

She risked another look. The girl stood near the end of the line, a tray now in her hands. Her gaze swept the crowded canteen, searching for a place to sit. It passed over full tables, over groups of laughing friends, and then it stopped on her. On Kelly, sitting alone. The girl started walking toward her table.

"Hi," a voice said, close enough to cut through the audiobook's narration. "Is this seat taken?"

Kelly didn't look up. She just nodded, once, a quick dip of her chin. The chair opposite her scraped against the linoleum. The scent of something fruity and sweet - strawberry shampoo, maybe - drifted over as the girl sat down. A carton rattled as she opened it, the sharp tear of foil punctuating the air.

Something green and mushy splattered against Kelly's left lens, obscuring her view of the table. She flinched this time, a full-body jolt that made her fork clatter against the plate.

The girl across from her twisted around. Kelly watched through the smeared lens as the boy at the front table threw his head back, laughing. He caught the girl's eye and puckered his lips, making an exaggerated kissing sound.

The girl turned back to Kelly, her eyebrows raised. "Are you going to let him get away with that?"

Kelly's fingers tightened around her fork. She could feel her heart beating faster, a frantic rhythm against her ribs. She looked down at her plate, at the peas scattered across it like green confetti. The headphones still pressed against her ears, but Davina Porter's voice seemed distant now.

"He's just a wanker," Kelly mumbled, the words barely audible. She pushed her glasses up her nose, wiping the smudge with her thumb. "Doesn't matter."

Mr. Henderson's hand landed on the boy's shoulder, gripping tight. He led him back to the table, his face a mask of stern disappointment. Kelly watched, her thumb hovering over the power button on her headphones. But Amy was standing up. Amy, who had sat with her. Amy, who had poured milk on a boy for her. Kelly pressed the button. The world rushed back in – the scrape of chairs, the whispers, the teacher's voice.

"Aye, right. What's all this, then?" Mr. Henderson demanded, his eyes moving from the boy's red face to Amy's calm one.

"She threw milk on me!" the boy shrieked, his voice cracking. "And then she smashed her fist in my face!"

Mr. Henderson looked at Amy. "Is that what happened?"

"He threw mashed potatoes at her," Amy said, her voice even, gesturing with a thumb toward Kelly. "Then he told me to come sit with him because I was 'too tidy' to be hanging around with a simpleton."

The boy's face went crimson. "You're a fucking liar!"

"When I went over to confront him about it," Amy continued, ignoring his outburst, "he started making verbal sexual advances toward me."

Mr. Henderson's jaw tightened. He looked back at the boy. "Liam?"

The boy's sneer returned, his eyes flicking to Kelly, who was now completely exposed without the sound-dampening headphones. He looked her up and down, a look of pure contempt on his face. "Shouldna wasted it on her," he said, his voice dripping with scorn. "No one'd even notice if you poured it on the wee simpleton's face. Probably get her wet, wouldn't it? The daftie likes that."

A sharp gasp escaped Kelly's lips. The air in her lungs turned to ice. Her whole body went rigid.

And then Amy moved.

It wasn't a wild swing. It was a single, straight punch, fast and economical. Her fist connected with the boy's throat. The sound was a sickening, wet crunch. The boy's eyes bulged, his hands flying to his neck as he staggered back a step. He made a horrible, gagging sound, a desperate, choked-off wheeze. Then his legs gave out and he collapsed to the floor in a heap, clawing uselessly at his own throat.

The canteen fell utterly silent. Mr. Henderson just stared, his mouth open, his face pale with shock. Amy stood over the boy, her breathing steady, her hands relaxed at her sides.

config.author_note:
[RHYTHM] Alternate short declaratives (3-7 words) with long flowing sentences (15-40 words). Put emotional payload at the END of long sentences. Use fragments for emphasis — max one per paragraph.

[SENSORY] Ground ALL emotion in physical sensation. Never write "she felt [emotion]" — write what the body does. Never name emotions even with qualifiers: not "something close to fear," not "a kind of dread." Show the body. The reader names the emotion. Primary senses: touch/temperature (horror), touch/proximity (romance), sight (fantasy), sound (literary). Include proprioceptive detail — where the body is in space.

[DIALOGUE] No adverb tags. Use action beats instead. Preserve complete speeches — do not trim dialogue for pacing. Write only surface meaning — let reader infer subtext. Each character needs: sentence length habit, vocabulary level, rhetorical tic, emotional default.

[CHARACTERS] No introduction paragraphs. Assemble from: how others react, what they notice, what they wear, ONE telling detail. Telling details imply danger, class, or character flaw directly: "slender as a knife." No interpretive clauses: not "dressed as if color might betray him."

[ENVIRONMENT] Setting has stance: hostile, indifferent, nurturing, witnessing, mirroring. Weather is symbolic. Nature is alive but NOT personified — "trees rustled like living things," not "the forest wanted to trap them."

[DESCRIPTION] One sharp detail > ten general ones. Specific materials (sable, not fur). Sensory adjectives (supple, gleaming). One moral judgment embedded ("soft as sin"). Cut after one telling detail.

[INTERNAL] Thought is action — processing, calculating, remembering. Never use thought for exposition. No metaphors for internal states — plain statement only. Veteran's memory pattern: past self → transformation → present contrast.

[PACING] Pressure stack: each beat adds ONE layer of tension. Never release early. Ground supernatural/extreme events in professional routine. End scenes on an IMAGE, not a statement. Do not inject dramatic beats that are not in the source — no sudden stops, no whirling, no sharp turns unless specified.

[BANNED PHRASES] primal | visceral | guttural | shatter (metaphoric) | wave of [emotion] | [emotion]-stricken | air forced from lungs | raw animal sound | "which had been [verb]ing" | "more [emotion] than any" | "her eyes widened in [emotion]" | "he felt a sense of" | "played on his lips" | "tugged at the corner of his mouth" | "danced in his eyes"

[BANNED TECHNIQUES] Em-dash repetition for emphasis ("But tonight—tonight"). Metaphors for internal states ("buried his fear like a corpse"). Injected dramatic beats not in source (stopped suddenly, whirled to face). Cliché physical descriptions (smirk played, smile tugged, laughter danced). Interpretive character descriptions. Abstract forces: "energy," "momentum," "weight," "pull," "anchor," "void." Internal summaries: "She realized," "She understood," "She knew."

[KELLY VOICE] Mild London accent. British terminology: mum, bloody, knickers, rubbish, brilliant, proper, mental. Direct, literal phrasing. When comfortable: longer sentences, animated, playful, "fuck" used frequently, self-deprecating humor. When stressed: short sentences, flat affect, possible selective mutism.

[CRITICAL] Do not explain. Do not summarize. Do not name emotions. Do not introduce characters directly. Do not use adverbs in dialogue tags. Do not release tension before climax. Do not inject drama. Do not use em-dash repetition. End on images.
